qa_pairs.question: How can a user reset their password for an Alliance national cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To reset your password for any Alliance national cluster, visit https://ccdb.alliancecan.ca/security/forgot. Please note that you will not be able to reset your password until your first role gets approved by Alliance staff.

qa_pairs.question: Why do text files created in a Windows environment sometimes cause problems when read in a Linux environment on an Alliance national cluster, and how can this be resolved?
qa_pairs.answer: Text files prepared in a Windows environment include an additional invisible "carriage return" character at the end of each line, which can cause issues when these files are read in a Linux environment. To resolve this, users should either create and edit their text files directly on the cluster using standard Linux text editors like `emacs`, `vim`, or `nano`, or use the command `dos2unix <filename>` on the cluster login node to convert the line endings to the appropriate convention.

qa_pairs.question: What do the 'Resources' and 'Priority' reasons signify when a user's job is in the pending (PD) state on an Alliance national cluster, and how can resource consumption be tracked?
qa_pairs.answer: When a job is in the pending (PD) state, 'Resources' means the cluster is currently very busy, and the job is waiting for available resources. 'Priority' indicates that the job is waiting due to its lower priority, often because the user or their research group has recently over-consumed their fair share of the cluster resources. Users can track their over- or under-consumption of resources using the `sshare` command, where the `LevelFS` column provides this information (a value less than one indicates over-consumption).
